name: Island of Circe
description: The Island of Circe, often identified as Aeaea, is a mythical island in Greek mythology, most famously known as the home of the sorceress Circe, daughter of Helios and Perse. This island is depicted as a lush, enchanting location, teeming with both natural beauty and lurking dangers. Circe, skilled in the arts of magic and potion-making, used her powers to transform visitors into animals, most notably turning Odysseus's crew into swine. Odysseus, aided by Hermes and the herb moly, managed to resist her magic and eventually became her lover, staying on the island for a year. The island represents temptation, danger, and the power of transformation. It's a place where mortals confront the boundaries between human and animal, and where the allure of pleasure can lead to peril. Aeaea is described with deep forests, hidden glades, and a mysterious atmosphere, reflecting the enigmatic nature of its inhabitant and the challenges she presents to those who venture onto its shores. The island's remoteness and Circe's isolation further emphasize its otherworldly quality, making it a significant location in the broader landscape of Greek myth. It stands as a reminder of the power of the gods and the unpredictable nature of fate.
subtitle: Home of the Enchantress Circe

keyMyths.title: Odysseus and Circe
keyMyths.description: Odysseus and his crew landed on Circe's island, where she transformed his men into swine. Odysseus, with Hermes' help, resisted her magic and stayed with her for a year.

keyMyths.title: Circe and Picus
keyMyths.description: Circe fell in love with Picus, but he rejected her. In her anger, she transformed him into a woodpecker.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Classic epic poem detailing Odysseus's journey home.
sources.text: Classic epic poem detailing Odysseus's journey home.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: A narrative poem recounting stories from Greek and Roman mythology.
